food_subgroup: Hot and cold tea-based beverages with milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 (milk tea) is the closest parent; no specific entry for Singaporean cold teh exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Tea, with milk) is the closest parent category; no specific cold-served Singaporean teh entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227493005 (Milk tea) is the closest available concept; no cold-specific Singaporean teh concept exists.

preparation_method: mixed with condensed or evaporated milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of cold Singaporean milk tea, approximately 250 ml, as typically served at a hawker centre or kopi tiam.

nutrient_source.title: Teh (Milk Tea with Condensed Milk) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists hot teh (milk tea with condensed milk) per 100 ml. Cold teh is the same beverage served chilled over ice; the nutrient composition per 100 g is treated as equivalent to the hot version. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo teh entry. Ice dilution is not accounted for in per-100g values as the HPB entry represents the beverage as consumed without ice dilution adjustment.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; milk tea and sweetened tea analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean cold teh specifically. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea / tea with milk and sugar (GI approximately 40–50, mean ~45), which is the closest analogous food. The moderate sugar content from condensed milk and the dampening effect of milk proteins on glycemic response support a low GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 10.35, rounded to 10.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~22 mg per 100 g; ~55 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Cold teh is low in sodium and does not meaningfully contribute to hypertensive risk; however, individuals with hypertension should be mindful of total added sugar intake from condensed milk, which may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar from condensed milk (~8.8 g sugar per 100 g; ~22 g per 250 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid cold teh prepared with condensed milk due to its significant added sugar load (~22 g per standard cup), which can cause postprandial blood glucose elevation; requesting reduced or no condensed milk, or substituting with evaporated milk and no added sugar, is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g) and low sodium; moderate sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cold teh has a low saturated fat and sodium profile, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk per serving; however, regular consumption of the sweetened condensed-milk version contributes to added sugar intake, which is associated with dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh (Cold Milk Tea) is a chilled version of the classic Singaporean kopi tiam milk tea, known locally as 'teh'. It is brewed from strong black tea — typically a robust Ceylon or Robusta-blend tea — and sweetened with condensed milk or a combination of evaporated milk and sugar, then served cold over ice. The beverage is a staple of Singapore's hawker centres and kopi tiams (traditional coffee shops), where it is ordered alongside meals or as a standalone refreshment. The cold version, sometimes called 'teh peng' (peng meaning ice in Hokkien), is especially popular in Singapore's tropical climate. The flavour profile is sweet, creamy, and malty, with the bitterness of the strong tea balanced by the richness of condensed milk. Nutritionally, cold teh provides approximately 52 kcal per 100 ml, with the majority of calories derived from carbohydrates (primarily added sugar from condensed milk, ~8.8 g per 100 g) and a small contribution from milk fat and protein. It is low in sodium and saturated fat but carries a moderate glycemic load per serving (~10.3 GL units for a 250 ml cup) due to its sugar content. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes are advised to moderate intake or request reduced sweetness. The beverage is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains dairy and is not suitable for those with lactose intolerance or milk allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cold milk tea Singapore?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for hot teh (milk tea with condensed milk), which is the closest approved primary source. Cold teh is nutritionally equivalent to hot teh per 100 g before ice dilution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea. No direct cold-teh-specific entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo hot teh entry; cold teh is the same beverage served chilled and is nutritionally equivalent per 100 g. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — tea and milk contain no dietary fibre). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in traditional teh preparation). 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — tea and condensed milk contain negligible vitamin C). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 7. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 8. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 9. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 10. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 11. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 12. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 13. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 14. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 15. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 16. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 17. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this beverage. 18. glycemic_index set to 45 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea); gi_category low. GL per serving = (45 × 9.2 × 250) / 100 / 100 = 10.35, rounded to 10.3. 19. GrabFood source restaurant (Hai Kah Lang - Funan Mall) and price data (SGD 5.67) are preserved from the input record for commercial context only and do not influence nutrient values. 20.
